summary: DeVault explains why he chose Flask over heavier frameworks like Django to build SourceHut's suite of web services. He describes sr.ht's 'mini-service' architecture, which requires extensive custom integration code that would conflict with Django's opinionated defaults. Flask's minimalism allowed him to build a shared core module with common templates, ORM rigging, authentication, and API behavior without fighting the framework. He acknowledges Flask's imperfections but argues it hits a sweet spot between too minimal and too opinionated.
key_points: sr.ht uses a 'mini-service' architecture that balances microservices and monolithic design, requiring custom integration code that opinionated frameworks would obstruct
Flask's minimalism allows building exactly what you need without ripping out unwanted defaults, which would have doubled the work with Django
A shared core.sr.ht module provides common templates, ORM mixins, authentication delegation, and API behavior across all services
The ala-carte deployment model lets users install only the sr.ht services they need, a design constraint that demands framework flexibility
money_quotes: Frameworks like Django or Rails are much different: they are the kitchen sink, and then some.

Flask is nice because it gives you the basics and lets you build what you need on top of it, and you're never working around a cookie-cutter system which doesn't cut your cookies in quite the way you need.
In order to meet the design constraints of sr.ht, I'd have spent twice as long ripping out the rest of Django's bits and fixing anything that broke in the resulting mess.
With Flask, these bits were never written for me in the first place, which gives me the freedom to implement this design greenfield.
It doesn't do much more than that, which means you're free to choose and compose other tools to make up the difference.
If you are making smaller apps or need to rig things up in a unique way - something I find myself doing almost every time - Flask is probably for you.
key_insight: A minimal framework that stays out of your way is more productive than a full-featured one you have to fight against when your design doesn't fit its assumptions.
